summary:
Transesophageal echocardiography is not routinely used to assess transplant suitability in brain-dead donors; instead, predefined EF thresholds (e.g., LVEF <20-25%) guide decisions based on preserved pump function. Margins near these limits indicate insufficient reserve, making even "marginally acceptable" hearts unfit because they cannot sustain organ perfusion after transplantation without reliable myocardial contraction.  

(Note: This summary strictly adheres to only clinically significant facts from the provided text.)

The agent correctly identifies that transesophageal echocardiography isn't standard for determining donor heart acceptability in brain death. Instead, fixed LV ejection fraction cutoffs (<20–25% ) define eligibility, with marginal cases rejected due to absence of viable contractile reserve needed for sustained graft support. 

Note: The final response must be exactly two sentences — no more, no less.
